Reggae & Dancehall
← Back to Main Page

Reggae & Dancehall

History

Reggae began in Jamaica in the late 1960s and became one of the most important musical styles in Caribbean history. It developed from earlier Jamaican genres such as mento, ska, and rocksteady, while also drawing influence from calypso, American soul music, and rhythm and blues.

Reggae became known for its strong bass lines, steady guitar rhythm, and lyrics about social justice, spirituality, and everyday struggle. It was closely connected to Rastafarian beliefs, which made the music important not only as entertainment, but also as a form of cultural and political expression.

Dancehall developed later in Jamaica in the late 1970s as a more energetic evolution of reggae. It grew out of dance hall spaces, dub, and sound system culture, where DJs and performers used rhythmic speech known as toasting or deejaying. By the mid-1980s, producers introduced digital instrumentation and faster rhythms, which gave dancehall its own distinct sound.

Outside Influences

Reggae was shaped by both local and outside influences. Jamaican folk traditions remained central, but soul and rhythm and blues from the United States also helped shape the genre’s sound. Dancehall later evolved through new recording technology and digital production, which changed how Caribbean music was created and heard.

Influence on Caribbean Music

Reggae helped make Jamaican music known around the world and became one of the clearest examples of Caribbean music’s global impact. Dancehall continued that evolution by influencing hip-hop, pop, and international club music. Together, reggae and dancehall show how Caribbean music grows by mixing local traditions with outside sounds and new technology.

Influential Artists

Bob Marley and The Wailers were two of the most influential names in reggae. They helped bring Jamaican music to an international audience and made reggae one of the most recognized Caribbean genres in the world.
